component_identification.content: Components include a map of Birmingham and surrounding locations, industry tiles for each player (cotton mill/manufactured goods, coal mine, iron works, brewery, pottery), link tiles (canals/rails), merchant tiles, cards (location and industry), loan and income tracks, money, beer barrels, coal and iron cubes, and player mats showing industry progression.

core_game_loop.content: Brass has two eras: Canal then Rail. On each turn, play two cards to take two actions, spending money as needed. Core actions are build, network, develop, sell, loan, and scout. Players build industries and links, consume shared market resources, and time flips (turning tiles over) for points and income growth. After Canal scoring, canals are removed and Rail era begins with stronger links and industries.

detailed_rules.content: Build requires either a matching location card for exact spot or industry card for a connected location of that industry. Some industry builds require coal, and rail links require both coal and beer; resources can be sourced from connected networks or market with rising costs. Iron is typically consumed immediately from any available source due to local distribution rules. Develop removes lower-level industry tiles from your board (often by spending iron), accelerating access to higher-value tiles. Sell flips certain industries by connecting to merchants and consuming beer, either your own connected beer or merchant beer if legal. Loans grant immediate cash but reduce income substantially, creating tempo versus efficiency tension. Overbuilding allows replacing your own industry tile or any level-1 industry under conditions, crucial for cycling weak tiles. Turn order each round follows current income ranking, making income timing important. At each era end, only flipped tiles and links score; unflipped industries generally do not contribute, so timing flips before scoring is essential.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Canal era scoring counts flipped canal-era industries and canal links, then canals are cleared. Rail era scoring counts flipped remaining industries and rail links, usually generating larger totals. Final score is cumulative VP from both scorings; highest VP wins with typical tiebreak by income then money.

beginner_strategy.content: Secure reliable access to coal/iron early so you are not forced into expensive market buys. Develop aggressively enough to reach efficient tiles before midgame, but avoid cash starvation. Treat beer as a tactical bottleneck: plan sells and rail builds around barrel availability. Build links that support your own flips first, then opportunistically connect to opponents resources. Loans are normal in opening turns; the skill is converting loan tempo into flipped tiles before each scoring.
